analysis.schema_warning: 240 unattributed kernels (12.6% of all kernels). Wall-time percentages for layer norm and attention preprocessing ops are understated. Re-run with --correlation-pass for improved attribution.

bottleneck.description: FP32 dtype forces cuBLAS to select the SIMT path (ampere_sgemm_*) rather than Tensor Core HMMA path (sm80_xmma_gemm_*). tensor_core_active_pct is 0.0 for aten::mm_0 and effectively 0 for aten::addmm_0 (0.248%). The fmha_cutlassF_f32 attention kernel runs at only 6.3% occupancy with 168 registers/thread and 24.5 MB local memory spills — all caused by the f32 kernel variant. Switching to BF16 routes all three operators to Tensor Core paths and replaces fmha_cutlassF_f32 with fmha_cutlassF_bf16, eliminating register spills.

transformation.description: Cast model parameters and input tensors to torch.bfloat16. On Ampere (A100), this routes aten::mm and aten::addmm from ampere_sgemm_* (SIMT FP32) to sm80_xmma_gemm_bf16bf16 (HMMA Tensor Core), and routes the attention kernel from fmha_cutlassF_f32_aligned_64x64_rf_sm80 to fmha_cutlassF_bf16, eliminating 24.5 MB of register spills and raising occupancy. A100 BF16 Tensor Core throughput is 312 TFLOPS vs ~19.5 TFLOPS for SIMT FP32 — theoretical 8-16x improvement on GEMM-bound operators. GPT-2 does not use tied embeddings (lm_head is a separate nn.Linear), so the shared-weight contraindication does not apply.
fx_steps: # Not an FX pass — apply before torch.compile().
# In get_model_and_input(), after constructing model and input tensors:
transformation.code_hint: Apply BEFORE torch.compile(). Dynamo traces dtype as a static property; changing dtype after compilation triggers a recompile. GPT-2 from HuggingFace: pass torch_dtype=torch.bfloat16 to from_pretrained() as an alternative to post-hoc .to(). Lower-risk fallback: torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32 = True before compilation — routes to TF32 (10-bit mantissa), ~3x speedup vs full BF16 ~8x, no dtype change required.

estimated_impact.notes: aten::mm_0 (51.93%) and aten::addmm_0 (15.68%) are compute-bound on SIMT. BF16 Tensor Core throughput is ~8-16x higher for these shapes (768x768, 512x3072, 512x2304). Estimated 50-60% total wall time reduction. The attention operator (3.18%) sees additional benefit from eliminating 24.5 MB local memory spills; fmha_cutlassF_bf16 runs at significantly higher occupancy than the f32 variant at 168 registers/thread.

operators: aten::mm_0 (336 kernels — FFN output projections and attention output projections)

bottleneck.description: Inductor emits aten.t() nodes immediately before aten.mm() calls, causing cuBLAS to perform an on-the-fly transposition of the weight matrix at every forward pass. GPT-2 Linear layers store weights in row-major (out_features, in_features) layout. cuBLAS receives a TN (Transposed, Non-transposed) problem, incurring an implicit DRAM reshape on every call. Pre-transposing weight buffers at model load time eliminates this overhead and allows cuBLAS to see a NN problem with contiguous strides. This is secondary to OPT-1 — BF16 dtype must be established first so the pre-transposed buffers match the runtime dtype of the mm inputs.

aten::mm_0.note: The '_nn' suffix confirms both operands are expected non-transposed by the selected kernel; the on-the-fly transpose happens in the cuBLAS dispatch layer before kernel selection

transformation.description: FX graph pass that detects aten.t() nodes feeding into aten.mm() or aten.addmm(), pre-transposes the corresponding weight parameter at graph-rewrite time, registers the transposed tensor as a new buffer, and rewrites the mm call to use the pre-transposed weight directly (no runtime aten.t()). Applies only to weights where the K dimension >= 512 to avoid memory overhead on small projections.
transformation.location: FX graph pass — apply to the compiled graph via torch.compile custom backend or as a post-Dynamo transformation
fx_steps: from torch.fx import Graph, GraphModule, Node
For each pattern:  mm(x, t(W))  or  addmm(b, x, t(W))
where W is a get_attr node (model parameter),
replace with:  mm(x, W_T)  where W_T = W.t().contiguous()
registered as a buffer on the GraphModule.
Only applies when W.shape[-1] >= 512 (1MB threshold for BF16 weights).
# Match aten.mm.default(x, t_node) or aten.addmm.default(b, x, t_node)
bias_node, x_node, t_node = node.args[0], node.args[1], node.args[2]
# t_node must be aten.t() wrapping a get_attr (weight parameter)
# Retrieve the actual weight tensor to check shape and create transposed copy
continue  # Skip small weights — memory overhead not worth it
# Pre-transpose and register as a new buffer
# Insert get_attr node for the new buffer before the current mm node
# Replace the mm/addmm node args — W_T is already transposed, no aten.t() needed
# Remove old aten.t() node if it has no other users
transformation.code_hint: Register this pass with a custom torch.compile backend using torch._dynamo.register_backend, or invoke it manually on a GraphModule returned by torch.export. Must run AFTER dtype promotion (OPT-1) so that W_T = weight_tensor.t().contiguous() produces a BF16 buffer matching BF16 mm inputs. Memory cost: one additional copy per weight matrix. For GPT-2-small, total additional weight memory is approximately 12 layers x (768x768 + 768x3072 + 3072x768 + 768x2304) x 2 bytes = ~540 MB. Ensure host has sufficient GPU memory before enabling.

estimated_impact.notes: Pre-transposition eliminates the implicit DRAM reshape in the cuBLAS TN dispatch path. For 768-dim and 3072-dim weight shapes, the transpose overhead is modest relative to the GEMM computation — estimated 3-8% additional reduction on top of OPT-1. The primary benefit is improved cuBLAS kernel selection: NN problems may select larger tile sizes than TN problems for these matrix dimensions.

bottleneck.description: Default torch.compile() uses a fixed heuristic for GEMM tile sizes and algorithm selection. For GPT-2's specific matrix shapes (e.g., [512, 768] x [768, 3072], [512, 2304] x [2304, 768]) on A100, the optimal cuBLAS algorithm index and CUTLASS tile configuration may differ from the default heuristic. max-autotune mode benchmarks multiple kernel candidates at compile time and selects the fastest, which is especially impactful after the BF16 dtype change since a new set of Tensor Core kernels becomes available.

aten::mm_0.note: 72.77% SM throughput is good but leaves ~27% headroom that better tile selection may recover

aten::addmm_0.note: 69.44% SM throughput — similarly sub-peak, consistent with sub-optimal tile heuristic

transformation.description: Switch from default torch.compile() to torch.compile(mode='max-autotune'). This enables Inductor's kernel benchmarking pass, which sweeps CUTLASS and cuBLAS algorithm variants for each unique (M, N, K) shape at compilation time and caches the fastest. No graph modification required.
fx_steps: # If using a custom backend wrapper (e.g., register_backend), pass mode through:
# Inside my_backend_fn, forward the mode kwarg to compile_fx:
transformation.code_hint: max-autotune significantly increases compile time (expect 5-15 minutes for GPT-2 small on first compile). Compilation result is cached to the Inductor cache directory. Set TORCHINDUCTOR_CACHE_DIR to a persistent path to avoid recompilation across runs. Also enable: torch.backends.cuda.matmul.allow_tf32 = True as a complementary setting (no-op after BF16 promotion but useful if OPT-1 is not applied). Combine with TORCH_COMPILE_DEBUG=1 to verify that max-autotune is selecting different kernels than the default heuristic.

estimated_impact.notes: Estimated 5-15% additional reduction on GEMM operators after OPT-1 BF16 promotion. The exact benefit depends on whether the heuristic-selected CUTLASS tile already happens to be optimal for these shapes on A100. Most impactful for the 512x768x3072 FFN shapes where multiple competitive tile configurations exist. No benefit for the attention operator (108 kernels, 3.18%) which is wave-starved due to small batch rather than tile selection.

global_notes: All ncu metric values from application-mode replay are 2-5x longer in absolute duration than wall-clock execution due to counter-collection overhead. Use duration values for relative operator comparison only, not for absolute latency estimation.

A100 SXM4 ridge point: 156 FLOP/byte for BF16 Tensor Core (312 TFLOPS / 2 TB/s). Post-BF16 promotion, aten::mm_0 shapes (e.g., [512, 768] x [768, 3072]) have arithmetic intensity ~200 FLOP/byte — above the ridge point, confirming compute-bound status will persist after dtype change. cuBLAS Tensor Core throughput is the new bottleneck, not memory.

12.6% unattributed kernels (240 kernels): dominant unattributed types are triton_per_fused_native_layer_norm_0 and triton_poi_fused__scaled_dot_product_efficient_attention_*. These represent layer norm and attention preprocessing costs not captured in the time_budget. Re-run with --correlation-pass to obtain accurate attribution and full wall time accounting.

GPT-2's combined QKV projection (c_attn: nn.Linear(768, 2304)) is already fused as a single 512x2304 GEMM. Standard QKV fusion (OPT pattern: wave_starvation on three separate mm nodes with shared input) does not apply here — the model architecture already implements this optimization.

The aten::_efficient_attention_forward_0 operator runs at only 6.30% achieved occupancy (waves_per_sm=1) with 168 registers/thread. This is a wave starvation issue driven by small batch size relative to the 108 SM A100. After BF16 promotion (OPT-1), the fmha_cutlassF_bf16 kernel variant uses fewer registers, which may allow more warps per SM, improving occupancy. If batch size can be increased, occupancy will improve proportionally.

aten::addmm_0 is classified 'well_optimized' by the triage with tensor_core_active_pct=0.248 — essentially 0%. This is a triage classification artifact: 0.248% Tensor Core activity on 456 SIMT kernels is negligible. The dominant kernel ampere_sgemm_128x32_nn is on the FP32 SIMT path. OPT-1 will fix this operator as well.

Dependency DAG: OPT-1 -> OPT-2 (BF16 dtype must be set before pre-transposing weights). OPT-3 is independent but most effective when applied after OPT-1 (autotune over BF16 Tensor Core kernels rather than FP32 SIMT kernels).
